content: Big O notation describes how an algorithm's runtime (or memory use) grows as the input size (n) grows — it's a way to compare algorithms independent of hardware. O(1) means constant time regardless of input size (e.g. accessing an array by index). O(n) means linear — time grows proportionally with input (e.g. scanning a list once). O(n²) means quadratic — common in nested loops over the same data (e.g. comparing every pair). O(log n) means logarithmic — the algorithm halves the problem each step (e.g. binary search). Big O describes the worst case unless stated otherwise, and ignores constant factors — O(2n) is still written O(n).

quiz.question: What is the time complexity of binary search on a sorted array?
Two nested loops, each running n times over the same array, give what complexity?
Why does binary search require a sorted array?
quiz.answer: Because it eliminates half the remaining elements each step by comparing against the middle — this only works if order is guaranteed.

content: A linked list is a linear data structure where each element (node) holds a value and a reference (pointer) to the next node, rather than sitting in contiguous memory like an array. This makes insertion/deletion at a known position O(1) (no shifting elements), but random access is O(n) since you must walk from the head. A singly linked list points only forward; a doubly linked list also points backward, allowing traversal both ways at the cost of extra memory per node.

quiz.question: What is the time complexity of accessing the 5th element of a singly linked list?
A doubly linked list uses less memory per node than a singly linked list.

content: A stack is a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) structure — the last item added is the first removed, like a stack of plates. Core operations: push (add to top) and pop (remove from top), both O(1). Stacks are used for undo functionality, expression evaluation, and function call tracking (the call stack). A queue is First-In-First-Out (FIFO) — the first item added is the first removed, like a line at a checkout. Core operations: enqueue (add to back) and dequeue (remove from front). Queues are used for task scheduling and breadth-first traversal.
example:
# Stack using a Python list
stack = []
stack.append(1)   # push
stack.append(2)
stack.pop()        # removes 2 (LIFO)

# Queue using collections.deque
from collections import deque
queue = deque()
queue.append(1)    # enqueue
queue.append(2)
queue.popleft()     # removes 1 (FIFO)

quiz.question: Which structure would you use to implement 'undo' in a text editor?
quiz.answer: A stack — push each action, pop to undo the most recent one.
